confirmed.title: Unvalidated `[package] entry` field enables module-path traversal out of lat_modules/ (arbitrary file load & code execution)
confirmed.trigger: A malicious package gets installed into lat_modules/<name>/ (downloaded from a malicious/compromised HTTPS registry via `clat install`, from a file:// registry, or a git dependency) and ships a lattice.toml whose `[package]` table sets e.g. `entry = "../../../../../../tmp/evil.lat"`, and omits both main.lat and src/main.lat. When the user program runs `import <name>`, pkg_resolve_module's Strategy 1/2 miss, so Strategy 3 reads the package's lattice.toml, takes meta.entry verbatim, and builds `lat_modules/<name>/../../../../../../tmp/evil.lat`, then `realpath()` resolves it to a path completely outside lat_modules/. That path is returned and the import loader (stackvm.c:7826 / eval.c:10326 / regvm.c) reads it with builtin_read_file and lexes/parses/executes it as Lattice source.
confirmed.consequence: Containment bypass: a third-party package controls which file an `import` resolves to, anywhere on the filesystem. This yields arbitrary file read (file content is consumed as source / leaked via parse errors) and arbitrary code execution if the attacker can also place a readable .lat at the traversed target (e.g. a world-writable dir). pkg_manifest_parse validates name/version/git refs (lines 696-718) but never validates meta.entry (read unchecked at line 644); pkg_resolve_module uses it directly in snprintf+realpath with no `..`/separator rejection.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/low] The code defect is real and reachable, but the claimed "high" impact is overstated.

REACHABILITY (confirmed real): src/package.c:644 reads `out->meta.entry = safe_strdup(map_get_str(pkg, "entry"))` with no validation (default "main.lat" at :648). The validator `pkg_path_component_is_safe` (:85-93, which rejects "..", "/", "\\", control bytes) is applied to dependency `version` and git refs at :699-701 but is NEVER applied to `meta.entry` — a genuine asymmetry. In `pkg_resolve_module` Strategy 3 (:1966-1976), when a package lacks main.lat and src/main.lat, the code reads the package's own lattice.toml, builds `lat_modules/<name>/<entry>` from the verbatim entry, and calls realpath() with NO containment/prefix check, returning the resolved path even if it escaped lat_modules/. The only guard in pkg_resolve_module (:1932) checks the import *name* for separators/leading-dot, not the entry field, so it does not block the trigger. All three import loaders (stackvm.c:7785-7826, eval.c:10345+, regvm.c:5632+) take that absolute path and read+lex+parse+execute it. So a package shipping `entry = "../../../../tmp/evil.lat"` would cause `import <name>` to load and run /tmp/evil.lat. I found no upstream guard preventing this.

IMPACT (overstated → low): The trigger requires an attacker-authored package already installed in lat_modules/<name>/ and then imported. In Lattice, `import` executes the module's top-level code (there is no declaration-only/sandboxed import), and the runtime exposes file-I/O builtins (builtin_read_file) to program code. Therefore importing a malicious package ALREADY yields arbitrary code execution and arbitrary file read via the package's own main.lat. The entry traversal grants no capability the attacker doesn't already have at the moment of import — it crosses no privilege boundary that isn't already crossed. The "arbitrary file read as source" via traversal is a strictly weaker primitive than the file-read the running package code already possesses. This makes it a defense-in-depth / containment-consistency gap (worth fixing to keep import resolution confined to lat_modules/, matching the dep validation right next to it), not the high-severity privilege escalation claimed. Hence real=true with severity corrected from high to low. || [high/low] The path-traversal mechanism is genuine and accurately described. src/package.c:644 reads `out->meta.entry = safe_strdup(map_get_str(pkg, "entry"))` with no validation; the validation block at lines 696-718 covers only dependency fields (d->name via pkg_name_is_valid, d->version via pkg_path_component_is_safe, git refs via strstr "..") and never touches meta.entry, even though pkg_path_component_is_safe (line 85) is right there. In pkg_resolve_module Strategy 3 (lines 1966-1976) the candidate is built as "lat_modules/<name>/<entry>" straight from dep_m.meta.entry with no ".." rejection; `name` is validated at line 1932 but `entry` is not. realpath() follows the traversal and the result is returned via strdup(resolved) with no check that it stays inside lat_modules/. The returned absolute path is consumed by the import loader (stackvm.c:7785-7826, also eval.c:10345 and regvm.c:5632): read via builtin_read_file, lexed, parsed, executed. So entry="../../../../etc/passwd" genuinely resolves and returns /etc/passwd if it exists. The finder did not misread ownership, bounds, types, or an existing check — the defect is real.

However, the claimed severity (high, "containment bypass", "arbitrary code execution") is greatly overstated and rests on a false premise that packages are sandboxed within lat_modules/. They are not. Lattice exposes read_file/write_file/read_file_bytes/write_file_bytes as unrestricted global natives to all code (runtime.c:4303-4306), and builtin_read_file (builtins.c:47) is a plain fopen(path) with no sandboxing. The trigger already requires an installed malicious package, which on import runs with full host privileges and can call read_file("/etc/passwd") directly or ship arbitrary code as its own main.lat (entry="evil.lat" needs no traversal at all). The traversal's only unique capability — reading a file outside the package dir and consuming its contents as source — is fully redundant with the globally available read_file. The net additional security capability over baseline is negligible. This is a legitimate missing-validation / consistency defect worth fixing (apply pkg_path_component_is_safe to meta.entry, or confine the realpath result to lat_modules/), but it is hardening rather than a high-severity vulnerability, hence corrected severity low. || [high/medium] I tried to refute this but could not: the path traversal is factually present and the finder's mechanism is accurate at every step.

VERIFIED FACTS:
1. src/package.c:644 reads `out->meta.entry = safe_strdup(map_get_str(pkg, "entry"))` with no validation; default is "main.lat" (line 648).
2. pkg_manifest_parse validates EVERY other path component fed to the filesystem — dependency name via pkg_name_is_valid, version/refs via pkg_path_component_is_safe, git tag/branch/rev via strstr(..,"..") (lines 699-701) — but conspicuously NEVER validates meta.entry. pkg_path_component_is_safe (lines 85-93) rejects "..", "/", "\\"; this guard is simply not applied to entry.
3. pkg_resolve_module Strategy 3 (lines 1966-1976) builds `candidate = lat_modules/<name>/<entry>` verbatim from the untrusted entry, then `realpath(candidate, resolved)` and returns strdup(resolved). I grepped the whole file: there is NO containment check (no strncmp/strstr against a lat_modules prefix) on the realpath result anywhere. An entry of "../../../../tmp/evil.lat" resolves to /tmp/evil.lat and is returned.
4. The returned absolute path flows into the import loader (stackvm.c:7785-7826, eval.c:10345, regvm.c:5632) which reads it via builtin_read_file and lexes/parses/executes it. Strategies 1/2 use fixed suffixes (main.lat, src/main.lat) so they cannot traverse; an attacker simply omits those files so resolution falls through to Strategy 3. The trigger is concretely reproducible.

So the traversal/missing-validation is real and unrefutable — I concede real=true.

SEVERITY DOWNGRADE (high -> medium): The finder frames the impact as "arbitrary code execution / containment bypass," which overstates it. Importing a lat_modules package already lexes/parses and EXECUTES that package's code (loader path above) — there is no sandbox around third-party packages. A malicious package author who can ship a lattice.toml can equally ship arbitrary .lat files inside their own package and gets full Lattice runtime capabilities on import regardless of entry traversal. Therefore the entry traversal is not a genuine privilege escalation for code execution; it is primarily a hardening/defense-in-depth gap. The only novel primitives it adds are: (a) loading a file from OUTSIDE the package directory, which still requires that file to exist (realpath fails on non-existent paths) and to be valid-ish Lattice to execute, and (b) a weak info leak where a non-Lattice target's content may surface in a lexer/parser error message (stackvm.c:7839 "import '%s': %s"). Both require the supply-chain precondition that a malicious package is already installed and being imported. It is a real bug the maintainers would clearly want to fix (they validate every other component), but its practical security impact is bounded — medium, not high.

confirmed.title: No CRLF/control-char sanitization in request line or header values -> HTTP request splitting/smuggling
confirmed.trigger:
A Lattice program builds a request from untrusted data, e.g. http_get("https://api.example.com/x?q=" + user_input) where user_input contains "
Header: val
...", or passes an attacker-influenced header value via http_post/http_request options.headers. http_parse_url (http.c:51-79) copies everything after the host verbatim into url->path (strdup(slash)) and never rejects CR/LF; format_request then writes method, path, host, and each header key/value directly into the wire request with no validation. The only 
 bytes the code emits are the literal separators -- embedded CR/LF in any of these fields is passed straight through.
confirmed.consequence: Request-line / header injection: an attacker who controls part of a URL path, query, method, or a header value can inject additional headers or smuggle a second request (request splitting/smuggling) to the upstream server, e.g. overriding Host, injecting a body, or poisoning a shared proxy/cache. Hardened HTTP clients (curl, Go net/http) reject embedded CR/LF in the request target and header values; this client does not.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/medium] Confirmed reachable with no sanitizing guard anywhere in the chain. The builtins http_get/http_post/http_request (runtime.c:4355-4357; eval.c:4087/4127/4206) take the URL string and options.headers map directly from Lattice values and copy them into HttpRequest with no validation (e.g. eval.c:4164-4166 hdr_vals[hi]=v->as.str_val; eval.c:4093-4100 url=args[0].as.str_val). Lattice strings can contain arbitrary bytes including CR/LF (file reads, prior network responses, interpolation of external data). http_parse_url copies the path verbatim — out->path = strdup(slash ? slash : "/") at http.c:79 — and only validates scheme/port, never rejecting CR/LF. format_request then writes the request line snprintf("%s %s HTTP/1.1
", method, path) at http.c:105 and each header "%s: %s
" at http.c:117 with no escaping; the only 
 handling in the file is in *response* parsing. net_tcp_write/net_tls_write are raw byte writes that do not transform the buffer. Concrete trigger: http_get("https://h/x?q=" + tainted) with tainted="a
Evil: 1" emits "GET /x?q=a
Evil: 1 HTTP/1.1
", injecting a header; likewise an attacker-controlled header value or method. So the path is fully reachable from untrusted input with no upstream validator — real=true. Severity: this is a CWE-93/CWE-113 missing-hardening issue in a client primitive (no in-process memory unsafety; impact is on the upstream server/proxy/cache and depends on the app forwarding untrusted data and a smuggling-susceptible downstream). Hardened clients (curl, Go net/http, Python http.client) explicitly reject embedded CR/LF, so medium is an appropriate rating; low would also be defensible given it requires application-level data flow. || [high/medium] The mechanism re-derives cleanly from the actual code — the finder did not misread ownership, bounds, types, or any existing check. End-to-end verification:

1. URL path: http_parse_url (src/http.c:35-81) splits scheme/host[:port]/path. The path is taken as everything from the first '/' and copied verbatim: `out->path = strdup(slash ? slash : "/")` (line 79). It validates only the port (lines 60-65) and empty host (line 72); there is NO check for CR/LF or control characters in the path/query. The query string is part of the path and is passed through unmodified.

2. Wire formatting: format_request (src/http.c:92-133) writes the request line `snprintf(... "%s %s HTTP/1.1
", req->method, url->path)` (line 105) and each header `snprintf(... "%s: %s
", req->header_keys[i], req->header_values[i])` (line 117) using bare `%s` with literal `
` separators. `%s` copies bytes up to the NUL terminator, so embedded 0x0D/0x0A in any field are written verbatim. The buffer `cap` (lines 94-98) is computed from the real `strlen()` of each field, so there is no truncation that would coincidentally block the injected bytes. No sanitization exists here either.

3. Sources of untrusted data: http_get/http_post/http_request (src/eval.c:4087-4278 and src/runtime.c:1235-1299) pass the program-supplied url, method, and header keys/values straight into HttpRequest with no filtering. A program doing `http_get("https://api.example.com/x?q=" + user_input)` with `user_input` containing `"
X-Injected: 1
"`, or passing an attacker-influenced `options.headers` value, injects directly into the wire request.

4. Transport: net_tcp_write (src/net.c) / net_tls_write (src/tls.c) just `send()` the raw bytes — no validation. A grep for crlf/sanitize/strpbrk/reject across src and include found nothing for the HTTP request path.

This is a textbook CWE-93 CRLF-injection / HTTP request splitting in the client. Hardened clients (curl, Go net/http) reject CR/LF in the request target and header values; this client does not. Severity medium is appropriate: it is a real injection/missing-defense issue, but exploitation depends on the Lattice application interpolating untrusted data into URL/method/headers (the standard threat model for this CWE), and impact (header override, Host poisoning, request smuggling to a shared proxy/cache) depends on the upstream peer. Confidence high — the absence of any sanitization is unambiguous in the code read. || [high/medium] Verified end-to-end; refutation failed. No CRLF/control-char sanitization exists anywhere in the HTTP request-building path. (1) http_parse_url (src/http.c:79) does out->path = strdup(slash ? slash : "/"), copying everything after the host verbatim with no CR/LF rejection; host is also extracted verbatim. (2) format_request (src/http.c:105,110-112,117) writes req->method, url->path, url->host, and each header key/value directly into the wire request via snprintf with literal 
 separators; CR/LF are not NUL so they pass through intact, and there is no validation. (3) The Lattice builtins feed untrusted data verbatim: http_request uses args[0]/args[1] as method/url unfiltered (src/eval.c:4250-4251), and http_post/http_request copy options.headers keys/values verbatim into hdr_keys/hdr_vals (src/eval.c:4147-4170, 4225-4248); runtime.c VM path is the same. (4) net_tcp_write/net_tls_write transmit raw bytes with no sanitization. A grep for iscntrl/strpbrk/CR-LF rejection/sanitize/length caps on the request side found nothing — the only 
 in the code are the separators it emits and response-side scans. Concrete trigger confirmed: http_get("https://api.example.com/x?q=" + user_input) with user_input = "foo\r\nX-Injected: 1" produces GET /x?q=foo\r\nX-Injected: 1 HTTP/1.1\r\n on the wire (Lattice strings support \r/\n escapes), injecting a header line; multiple CRLFs allow smuggling a second request. Same via the method arg or any options.headers value. This is CWE-93/CWE-113 (CRLF injection / HTTP request-splitting/smuggling). Hardened clients (curl, Go net/http, Python http.client) reject embedded CR/LF; this one does not. Severity medium is appropriate: it is an injection/missing-defense issue (not memory corruption or RCE) that requires the application to route untrusted data into a request field, but it enables real header injection, Host override, cache poisoning, and request smuggling against upstreams/shared proxies.

confirmed.title: Undersized buffer for "Wrap in try/catch" code action truncates the produced edit for deeply-indented lines (indent_len*3 vs required indent_len*5)
confirmed.trigger: A textDocument/codeAction request whose context.diagnostics contains a diagnostic whose message matches the try/catch trigger words ("throw", "exception", "uncaught", "Unhandled error", ...) and whose range.start.line points at a source line indented with more than 10 leading whitespace bytes. The diagnostics array is fully client-controlled. The format string substitutes `indent` 5 times (try line, body line, catch line, comment line, closing-brace line) plus `original` once, but `new_len` only budgets `indent_len * 3`. Once `indent_len >= 11`, allocated size (indent_len*3 + strlen(original) + 64) is smaller than the required size (indent_len*5 + strlen(original) + ~43).
confirmed.consequence: The returned WorkspaceEdit's newText is silently truncated by snprintf (e.g. the closing `}` / catch block is dropped), so applying the quick fix yields malformed/unbalanced source. This is a correctness bug only: snprintf is bounded by new_len, so there is NO heap overflow or OOB write, and the return value is not used to advance a pointer.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/low] Verified against src/lsp_server.c:2814-3020. In handle_code_action, `msg` (line 2852) is the client-supplied context.diagnostics[i].message, `diag_line` (2854-2859) is the client-supplied diagnostic range.start.line, and doc->text is the buffer set by the client's didOpen/didChange — all untrusted/client-controlled. The try/catch branch (2978-2979) triggers on any of "throw"/"exception"/"uncaught"/"Uncaught"/"unhandled error"/"Unhandled error" appearing in the message, with no upstream guard. get_line_text (2752) and the indent-counting loop (2985-2987) impose no cap on leading whitespace. The format string at 3006-3007 substitutes `indent` 5 times and `original` once; literal bytes = 42 (+1 NUL = 43), so required size = indent_len*5 + strlen(original) + 43, but new_len = indent_len*3 + strlen(original) + 64 (line 3003). Truncation occurs once 2*indent_len > 21, i.e. indent_len >= 11. At indent_len=11 with empty original, required=98 vs allocated=97, so snprintf silently drops the trailing `}`; deeper indentation drops more of the catch block. indent_len>=11 is reachable even by benign code (~3 levels of 4-space indent = 12 bytes, or 11 tabs), so a real codeAction request reaches it. Consequence is correctness only: snprintf is length-bounded by new_len and make_text_edit copies the NUL-terminated truncated string via cJSON_AddStringToObject — no heap overflow or OOB write, and the return value isn't used to advance any pointer. Applying the quick fix yields unbalanced/malformed source. Low severity confirmed (cosmetic/correctness defect in an LSP quick-fix edit, user-visible and undoable, no memory-safety impact). || [high/low] Mechanism re-derived from the actual code in src/lsp_server.c and it holds. At line 3003 the allocation is `size_t new_len = indent_len * 3 + strlen(original) + 64;`. The snprintf format at line 3006, `"%stry {\n%s  %s\n%s} catch (e) {\n%s  // handle error\n%s}"`, substitutes `indent` 5 times (try line, body-indent, catch line, comment line, closing-brace line) and `original` once. The literal (non-%s) characters total 42 (`try {`=5, `\n`=1, `  `=2, `\n`=1, `} catch (e) {`=13, `\n`=1, `  // handle error`=17, `\n`=1, `}`=1) plus the NUL = 43. Required size is therefore indent_len*5 + strlen(original) + 43, while allocated is indent_len*3 + strlen(original) + 64. allocated - required = -2*indent_len + 21, which goes negative exactly when indent_len >= 11 (at indent_len=11 it is short by 1 byte, dropping the trailing `}`; larger indents drop more of the catch block). The finder's "indent_len*3 vs indent_len*5" and ">10 leading whitespace bytes" thresholds are precisely correct.

Trigger is reachable and matches the claim: in lsp_handle_code_action the diagnostics array comes from client-controlled context.diagnostics (line 2845); `msg` is the diagnostic message and `diag_line` its start line. The try/catch branch fires when msg contains "uncaught/Unhandled error/throw/exception" (line 2978-2979). `line_text`/`indent_len` are computed from the real document line at diag_line (lines 2982-2987) with no cap on indentation, so a deeply-indented line (e.g. 12-space, 3 levels of 4-space indent) plus a matching diagnostic reproduces it. Such diagnostics ("uncaught exception", "may throw") are plausible from the Lattice analyzer, so this is reachable in normal operation, not just adversarially.

I confirm the finder's characterization that this is NOT a memory-safety issue: snprintf is bounded by new_len, so it truncates safely with no heap overflow or OOB write, and the return value isn't used to advance any pointer. The only consequence is a malformed/unbalanced WorkspaceEdit newText (the closing brace / catch block silently dropped) when applying the "Wrap in try/catch" quick fix. That is a genuine correctness bug but cosmetic/low-impact, so the low severity is appropriate. || [high/low] Confirmed by reading src/lsp_server.c. In handle_code_action (line 2814), the loop at 2848 iterates over context.diagnostics (line 2845), which is fully client-controlled. For each diagnostic, msg (2852) and diag_line (2856-2858) come from the client. The try/catch code action (2978-2979) fires when msg contains "uncaught"/"Unhandled error"/"throw"/"exception". get_line_text (2752) returns the document line minus newline, and the indent scan (2986) counts leading spaces/tabs with no upper clamp, so a line with >=11 leading whitespace bytes gives indent_len>=11.

The buffer math at 3003 is genuinely undersized. The format string at 3006 substitutes `indent` 5 times and `original` once, with 42 literal bytes, so the full output needs 5*indent_len + strlen(original) + 43 bytes (incl. NUL). The allocation is only 3*indent_len + strlen(original) + 64. Allocated minus required = 21 - 2*indent_len, which is non-negative only for indent_len <= 10. At indent_len >= 11 (e.g. a 12-space / 3-level-indented line, common in real code), snprintf silently truncates the produced edit, dropping the catch block / closing brace and yielding malformed source when applied.

I attempted to refute this and could not: there is no clamp on indent_len, no length validation, and the diagnostics array is attacker/client-controlled. However, the impact is correctness-only: malloc(new_len) + snprintf(new_text, new_len, ...) is strictly bounded, the return value is not used to advance any pointer, and there is no heap overflow or OOB write. This exactly matches the finder's stated consequence. Severity low is appropriate (silent truncation of an LSP quick-fix edit, no memory safety impact).

confirmed.title: Unchecked format_request() return dereferenced via strlen() before send
confirmed.trigger: format_request() returns NULL when its malloc(cap) fails (http.c:100-101). http_execute() stores the result in raw_req but never checks it for NULL before calling strlen(raw_req), so an allocation failure leads directly to strlen(NULL).
confirmed.consequence: NULL-pointer dereference / crash (DoS). Allocation-failure gated, so not remotely triggerable on its own, but it is an unchecked-return defect on the request path; it also leaks the open socket fd and the parsed HttpUrl since the function would crash rather than reach the cleanup paths.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/low] Code-level claim is accurate. format_request() (src/http.c:92-133) returns NULL on malloc failure (lines 100-101). http_execute() at src/http.c:332-338 assigns the result to raw_req and immediately passes strlen(raw_req) to net_tls_write/net_tcp_write with no NULL check — strlen is evaluated before the write call, so a NULL return is an unconditional crash. The omission is inconsistent with the surrounding defensive style (format_request checks its own malloc; resp_buf malloc is checked at lines 351-352; parse_response checks its allocations). So this is a genuine unchecked-return null-deref defect, not a false positive — real=true.

However, on the reachability axis the trigger is OOM-only and NOT reachable from untrusted input. The sole way format_request returns NULL is malloc(cap) failing. cap and all request fields (method, url, headers, body) come from the Lattice program constructing the outbound request — callers are src/package.c:306/408, src/eval.c:4102/4181/4259, and src/runtime.c:1244/1270/1293, all of which build the HttpRequest from program-controlled values. This is a client-side request builder; no untrusted-input vector (LSP request, archive entry, registry/HTTP response, TLS peer, source comment) flows into the malloc-failure condition, and an attacker cannot force malloc to fail. The finder concedes it is allocation-failure gated and not remotely triggerable.

Verdict: a real but extremely-low-impact robustness/unchecked-return defect that only manifests under memory exhaustion; correctly rated low severity. The secondary fd/HttpUrl-leak consequence is moot on crash, and the codebase already leaks fd/url on the adjacent resp_buf OOM path (lines 351-352), so OOM cleanup is not rigorously handled here regardless. || [high/low] Mechanism verified against actual source. In src/http.c, format_request() (line 92) returns NULL on its sole failure path: `char *buf = malloc(cap); if (!buf) return NULL;` (lines 100-101). In http_execute(), line 332 stores this in raw_req, and lines 334-338 call `net_tls_write(fd, raw_req, strlen(raw_req), err)` / `net_tcp_write(fd, raw_req, strlen(raw_req), err)` with NO NULL check between line 332 and the strlen() calls. strlen() of a NULL pointer is an unconditional NULL dereference, and since strlen(raw_req) is evaluated as a function argument it executes before net_*_write could ever inspect the pointer, and before the tolerant `free(raw_req)` at line 339. So the claim is accurate: unchecked-return defect leading to strlen(NULL). The finder did not misread ownership, bounds, or an existing check — there is genuinely no guard. The consequence (crash/DoS, and not reaching the cleanup at lines 341-345 that closes fd and frees the HttpUrl) also holds. Trigger is correctly characterized as allocation-failure (OOM) gated and not remotely controllable, since cap is always >=256 and malloc failure is the only NULL-producing path. Notably the surrounding code DOES check other allocations (e.g. line 352 `if (!resp_buf) return NULL;`), confirming this missing check is an inconsistency rather than an intentional invariant. Real bug; low severity is appropriate because it only manifests under allocation failure. || [high/low] Confirmed by reading src/http.c directly. format_request() (http.c:92-133) returns NULL at line 101 when malloc(cap) fails. In http_execute(), line 332 stores the result in raw_req, and lines 335/337 call strlen(raw_req) with no intervening NULL check. The if (!ok) guard at line 341 only fires after the writes complete, far too late. Because strlen(raw_req) is evaluated as a function argument before net_tls_write/net_tcp_write is entered, no NULL handling inside those callees can prevent the deref — strlen(NULL) is dereferenced first, causing a crash. I actively looked for a guard, an early-return, or callee tolerance and found none; the surrounding code even checks other mallocs (e.g., line 154, line 352), confirming this is a genuine omission rather than a safe-by-construction pattern. The trigger is allocation failure only, so it is not remotely/attacker controllable, which justifies the low severity (an OOM-gated DoS / unchecked-return null-deref). The noted fd and HttpUrl leaks are real but moot since the crash precedes cleanup. Could not refute; the defect is real.

confirmed.title: Socket fd and parsed URL leaked when response buffer allocation fails
confirmed.trigger: After a successful connect()/write, http_execute allocates the 8 KiB response buffer. If this malloc fails, it returns NULL immediately without calling net_tcp_close(fd)/net_tls_close(fd) or http_url_free(&url).
confirmed.consequence: File-descriptor leak plus heap leak (url.scheme/host/path) on the OOM path. The same omission exists in parse_response's OOM returns (http.c:173,175,233,285) which leak the partially-built HttpResponse and its header arrays. Resource exhaustion under memory pressure; OOM-gated.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/low] VERIFIED IN CODE, but reachability from untrusted input is essentially nil. The leak is accurately described: at src/http.c:351-352 `char *resp_buf = malloc(resp_cap); if (!resp_buf) return NULL;` returns without `net_tcp_close(fd)`/`net_tls_close(fd)` or `http_url_free(&url)`. At that point `fd` is a real open socket (opened lines 316-320) and `url` holds heap strings scheme/host/path (http_url_free at line 83 frees them). Every other error path in http_execute (322-345, 368-372) performs this cleanup, so the omission on the malloc-NULL path is a genuine fd+heap leak. The parse_response OOM returns (154, 173, 175, 233, 285) likewise leak the partial HttpResponse/header arrays. So the defect physically exists — real=true.

Reachability assessment (my role): the trigger for ALL of these is allocator failure, never untrusted-input content. Line 351 is a FIXED 8192-byte allocation independent of any server response. parse_response's resp (calloc 153) and header arrays (16 pointers, 172/174) are fixed/tiny; the body mallocs (233 chunked, 285 plain) are sized by body_len, which is bounded by raw_len = bytes actually received into resp_buf (the chunked path at 255-256 already caps to_copy to `avail`, preventing attacker-claimed sizes from inflating the allocation). A malicious HTTP server or TLS peer therefore cannot drive any of these mallocs to fail via response content — only genuine host memory exhaustion can. There is no input-validator gate to bypass because input never controls the trigger.

Impact when reached: one fd plus a few dozen bytes of strings leaked at the exact instant the process is already OOM and unwinding with NULL; it is not repeatedly/amplifiably triggerable by a remote party (each trigger needs a fresh independent OOM event). This is a real but trivial error-path cleanup gap, consistent with the claimed 'low' / 'OOM-gated' rating. I keep real=true because the leak is genuine and accurately cited, but flag that it is NOT reachable from untrusted input and has negligible practical/security value — borderline informational. || [high/low] Mechanism confirmed by reading the actual code. In http_execute (src/http.c:308-410), after a successful connect (fd >= 0, checked at 321) and write (ok at 341), the response buffer is allocated at http.c:351-352: `char *resp_buf = malloc(resp_cap); if (!resp_buf) return NULL;`. On malloc failure this returns NULL without calling net_tcp_close/net_tls_close(fd) or http_url_free(&url). fd is a real OS socket — net_tcp_close (src/net.c:274-283) calls close(fd), so it is the sole owner; skipping it leaks the descriptor. url was filled by http_parse_url (http.c:35-81) with strdup'd scheme/host/path, freed only by http_url_free (http.c:83-88); skipping it leaks ~3 heap strings. The two sibling failure paths both clean up correctly: write-fail at 341-346 does close+http_url_free, and read-fail-no-data at 368-372 does free(resp_buf)+close+http_url_free. Only the line-352 path omits cleanup — a genuine asymmetry, not a misread of an existing check or ownership.

The secondary parse_response claims also hold: resp is calloc'd at http.c:153; the OOM returns at 173 (!header_keys), 175 (!header_values), 234 (chunked body !body, malloc at 233), and 285 (non-chunked !resp->body) all `return NULL` without freeing resp or the already-allocated header arrays/strndup'd header strings, and the caller holds no reference, so they leak. (The realloc-grow case at 189-194 does free correctly and was correctly not cited.)

All paths are OOM-gated (malloc/calloc/realloc failure), making this a real but rare leak — low severity is appropriate. No TLS-verification or bounds error was misattributed; the leak mechanism is exactly as described. || [high/low] Confirmed by reading src/http.c. At http_execute (http.c:348-352), when malloc(resp_cap=8192) fails, the code does `if (!resp_buf) return NULL;` with no cleanup. At that point fd is a valid open socket (it passed `if (fd < 0)` at line 321 and the write succeeded — the write-failure early return at 341-346 was not taken) and url was successfully parsed at line 310, so url.scheme/host/path are strdup'd heap strings (lines 40/43,67,79) that http_url_free (83-88) is responsible for freeing. Returning NULL here leaks the socket fd (no net_tcp_close/net_tls_close) and the three url strings (no http_url_free). The intended cleanup pattern is unambiguous because every other error path in the same function performs it: connect-fail at 322-323 frees url; write-fail at 341-346 closes fd and frees url; read-fail at 368-372 frees resp_buf, closes fd, and frees url; normal exit at 401-404 closes fd and frees url. Line 352 simply omits all of it. The secondary claim is also accurate: parse_response's OOM `return NULL`s at 173 (leaks calloc'd resp), 175 (leaks resp + header_keys), 233, and 285 (leak resp plus populated header arrays and key/value strings). I attempted to refute — there is no guard, no possible caller-side recovery (fd and url are function locals never exposed), and no guarantee malloc cannot fail — so the bug stands. It is purely OOM-gated and each occurrence leaks one fd plus a handful of small allocations, so low severity is appropriate. real=true.

confirmed.title: No upper bound on total HTTP response size read into memory
confirmed.trigger: http_execute reads the entire response into resp_buf with an unbounded doubling loop and no maximum-size cap. A malicious or compromised server -- including the package registry contacted by registry_fetch_versions/registry_download_package, or any host passed to http_get -- can keep streaming data (refreshing the per-recv SO_RCVTIMEO window each read) until the client's memory is exhausted.
confirmed.consequence: Memory-exhaustion denial of service driven by an untrusted/attacker-controlled server response. Unlike net_tcp_read_bytes (which got a NET_READ_BYTES_MAX cap) and the chunked decoder (which clamps to available bytes), the top-level response accumulation has no size ceiling.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/low] Confirmed by reading the real code. src/http.c:348-409 (http_execute) reads the entire HTTP response into resp_buf via an unbounded doubling-realloc loop (the cited lines 380-390). Loop termination conditions are only: read error, zero-length chunk/EOF (line 375), or realloc failure (line 383) — there is NO maximum-response-size cap. The single SO_RCVTIMEO set on the socket (net.c:331) is a per-recv timeout, not a total deadline, so a server that drips at least one byte per timeout window keeps net_tcp_read/net_tls_read returning >0 indefinitely while resp_buf doubles toward OOM.

Reachability from untrusted input is clearly established: http_execute is invoked by the exposed Lattice builtins http_get/http_post/http_request (eval.c:4087-4102, runtime.c:4244-4355, http module in runtime.c:4013) — the URL and thus the remote server are script/attacker-selectable, and the response body is entirely server-controlled. It is also invoked by registry_fetch_versions (package.c:306) and registry_download_package (package.c:408) during package install, where the package registry is a genuine untrusted/compromisable third-party server (trivially MITM-able for http:// URLs). No upstream guard limits response size: parse_response (http.c:137) only rejects empty responses, and there is no Content-Length-based bound anywhere. The claim's contrast is accurate — net_tcp_read_bytes has NET_READ_BYTES_MAX=256MiB (net.c:193,230) and the chunked decoder clamps to_copy to available bytes (http.c:255-256), but the top-level accumulation has no equivalent ceiling.

This is a genuine memory-exhaustion DoS. Severity 'low' is appropriate: impact is limited to single-process memory exhaustion (the realloc-failure path at line 383 breaks gracefully, so on a 64-bit host with a per-process memory limit it may simply error rather than crash the host), it requires the victim to initiate the request and an actively malicious/compromised or MITM'd server, and there is no memory corruption or code execution. Keeping severity at low. || [high/low] Verified the mechanism against the real code, not the finder's snippet. In src/http.c:308-410 `http_execute` reads the HTTP response in a `for(;;)` loop (lines 354-399) that appends each chunk into `resp_buf`. The growth is a doubling `while (resp_len + clen + 1 > resp_cap)` loop (lines 380-390) whose only stop conditions are integer-overflow of the doubling (`newcap > resp_cap`), realloc failure, EOF (`clen==0`), or a read error. There is NO maximum-size cap on `resp_cap`/`resp_len`, so a server that keeps streaming data forces unbounded growth until the allocator fails (gigabytes of RSS / OOM-kill).

Per-chunk reads are individually bounded — `net_tcp_read` reads at most TCP_READ_BUF=8192 (src/net.c:188,207) and `net_tls_read` at most SCHAN_READ_BUF (src/tls.c:438-475) — but the outer accumulation loop runs an unbounded number of iterations. The timeout claim is also correct: `net_tcp_set_timeout` sets SO_RCVTIMEO (src/net.c:310-332), a per-recv idle timeout, not a total transfer deadline, so a server that streams steadily never trips it.

The finder's comparison points check out and demonstrate inconsistent hardening: `net_tcp_read_bytes` enforces NET_READ_BYTES_MAX = 256 MiB (src/net.c:193,230), and the chunked decoder clamps copies to `avail` rather than the attacker-claimed chunk_size (src/http.c:255-256), yet the top-level response accumulation has no ceiling. Attack surface confirmed: http_execute is reachable from program-controlled `http_get`/`http_post` (src/runtime.c:1244, src/eval.c:4102) and from the package-registry paths registry_fetch_versions (src/package.c:306) and registry_download_package (src/package.c:408).

No misread of ownership, bounds, types, or an existing check — the missing cap is real. It is a memory-exhaustion DoS that requires an untrusted/compromised server and only affects the client process, so low severity (defense-in-depth/hardening) is appropriate rather than higher. || [high/low] Confirmed by reading src/http.c:348-399 and the net read primitives. http_execute reads the response in an unbounded `for(;;)` loop, calling net_tcp_read (net.c:195, max 8192 B/call) or net_tls_read (tls.c:107/438, one read-buffer/call) and accumulating each chunk into resp_buf via the doubling loop at lines 380-390. There is NO maximum-total-size ceiling. The only loop-exit conditions are a NULL chunk (read error), an empty chunk (EOF, clen==0), or realloc failure — none of which a cooperative attacker server triggers while it keeps streaming bytes.

Refutation attempts all fail: (1) net_tcp_set_timeout sets SO_RCVTIMEO, a per-recv idle timeout that is refreshed every read, so a server sending data continuously never times out and total bytes are unbounded; (2) Content-Length is never consulted by the read loop (read-until-EOF); (3) the `newcap > resp_cap` test only guards size_t overflow of the doubling, not total footprint, so memory is exhausted long before overflow; (4) the memcpy is safe (clen<=8192, inner while guarantees resp_cap>=resp_len+clen+1), so the sole consequence is unbounded heap growth, i.e., exactly the claimed memory-exhaustion DoS. The server is untrusted for the exposed http_get builtin and for registry_fetch_versions/registry_download_package (a compromised registry).

Tellingly, the same file/module shows the maintainers treat unbounded reads as a security concern: net.c:190-233 added NET_READ_BYTES_MAX (256 MiB, with an explicit security comment) to net_tcp_read_bytes, and the chunked decoder clamps to available bytes — yet this top-level accumulation lacks any analogous cap. The finding is real. Severity 'low' is appropriate: exploitation requires the contacted server to be malicious/compromised and to stream gigabytes, the blast radius is the client process itself, and https connections are MITM-protected by TLS; it is a defense-in-depth/consistency gap rather than a high-impact vulnerability.

confirmed.title: Binary response bodies silently truncated at first NUL byte (corrupts archive/tarball downloads)
confirmed.trigger: net_tcp_read/net_tls_read return a NUL-terminated C string (net.c:214 buf[n]='\0'); http_execute then measures each chunk with strlen(). Any 0x00 byte in the TCP stream terminates the measured length, so all bytes after the first NUL in that recv() are dropped before they ever reach resp_buf. registry_download_package (package.c:408,460) downloads package payloads this way and writes resp->body to disk as the package source.
confirmed.consequence: Any response containing a NUL (i.e. essentially every binary tarball/archive) is silently truncated, despite parse_response carefully tracking an exact body_len. Downloaded package archives are corrupted/incomplete, and bodies are not byte-accurate. Data-integrity bug on the untrusted archive-download path; not memory-unsafe.
confirmed.verifier_notes:
[high/low] Confirmed by reading the cited code. net_tcp_read (net.c:207-214) does `recv(fd,buf,TCP_READ_BUF,0)` then `buf[n]='\0'` and returns only a `char *` — no byte count is propagated. net_tls_read (tls.c:119-126) is identical (`SSL_read(...)` + `buf[n]='\0'`). http_execute's read loop at http.c:374 then measures each chunk with `size_t clen = strlen(chunk)`, so any 0x00 in the recv'd block undercounts the chunk; post-NUL bytes are never memcpy'd into resp_buf (line 395) and are lost permanently (already drained from the kernel buffer). Worse, a chunk beginning with NUL hits `if (clen==0) break;` (http.c:375-378) and is misinterpreted as EOF, terminating the whole response read. The exact-tracking body_len in parse_response cannot help because resp_buf is already truncated before parse_response (http.c:407) runs.

Reachability is unambiguous and from genuinely untrusted input: http_execute is exposed to Lattice programs as the builtins http_get/http_post/http_request (runtime.c:1235-1299, calls at 1244/1270/1293; also eval.c:4102/4181/4259), so any program fetching a binary URL is affected. The package manager's registry_download_package (package.c:408) downloads package payloads through this same path and writes resp->body to disk as main.lat/lattice.toml (package.c:451/460), with a comment explicitly anticipating tarball storage. I searched for upstream guards and found none — recv()/SSL_read() return raw bytes including NUL with no validation, and the whole HTTP stack is C-string-based (HttpResponse.body is `char *`), so nothing rejects or escapes a NUL. The only precondition is a response body containing a 0x00 byte, which is true of essentially all binary archives (gzip magic 0x1f8b, tar/zip padding). This is a real, reachable, silent data-integrity bug. It is not memory-unsafe and the dominant real-world HTTP usage is text (JSON/HTML where NUL is absent), so low severity is appropriate. || [high/low] Mechanism re-derived from actual code and holds. net_tcp_read (src/net.c:195-216) and net_tls_read (src/tls.c:107-128) both NUL-terminate (buf[n]='\0') and return only a char* with no length out-parameter — confirmed by signatures in include/net.h:31 and include/tls.h:20. http_execute (src/http.c:374) therefore measures each recv chunk solely via `size_t clen = strlen(chunk);`, then memcpy's only `clen` bytes (line 395). Consequences: (a) any 0x00 inside a chunk drops all following bytes in that chunk; (b) a chunk that begins with 0x00 yields clen==0, which lines 375-378 treat as EOF, breaking the loop and discarding the rest of the response. parse_response's body_len (src/http.c:219) is computed from the already-truncated resp_len, so its 'exact' length describes the corrupted buffer — the finder's framing is accurate. Reachable via package downloads (src/package.c:306,408 writing resp->body to disk) and user-facing http_get/http_post/http_put (src/eval.c:4102/4181/4259, src/runtime.c). The finder did not misread ownership, bounds, or an existing guard — there is no length-aware path; the count from recv()/SSL_read() is genuinely thrown away. Additional downstream NUL-truncation exists (builtin_write_file's const char* content at package.c:460; value_string(hresp->body) at eval.c:4109), which reinforces rather than negates the finding. Not memory-unsafe (no OOB/UAF); pure binary-data-integrity bug. 'low' is appropriate, especially since the registry path currently treats bodies as text and full tarball handling is unimplemented. || [high/low] Confirmed by reading the actual code. The read primitives are length-lossy: net_tcp_read (src/net.c:195-216) does `ssize_t n = recv(fd, buf, TCP_READ_BUF, 0); buf[n]='\0'; return buf;` and net_tls_read (src/tls.c:107-128) does `int n = SSL_read(...); buf[n]='\0'; return buf;`. Both return a bare `char *` and DISCARD `n` — there is no length out-parameter. Consequently http_execute (src/http.c:374) must recover length with `size_t clen = strlen(chunk);`. This truncates at the first embedded 0x00: bytes after the first NUL in a recv'd chunk never reach resp_buf, and if a chunk begins with 0x00 (`clen == 0`, http.c:375-378) the loop breaks treating it as EOF, dropping that recv and all subsequent data. parse_response computes body_len from the already-truncated resp_len (http.c:219), so its exact body tracking cannot recover the lost bytes. The package download path confirms a concrete trigger: registry_download_package (src/package.c:412 calls http_execute, 451/460 write resp->body) persists the body via builtin_write_file (src/builtins.c:75-83), which is itself NUL-unsafe — `fputs(content, f)` stops at the first NUL and the file is opened in text mode "w". I searched for a binary-safe alternative: net_tcp_read_bytes/net_tls_read_bytes do track `total`, but they STILL return char* with `buf[total]='\0'` and are not used by http_execute. No guard, length field, or Buffer path exists on the HTTP body path. The bug is genuinely reachable and not refutable; it is broadly triggerable (any binary tarball, and any gzip/deflate-encoded response, which contains NULs constantly). It is a data-integrity/correctness defect, not memory-unsafe. Severity low is appropriate because truncated downloads fail loudly downstream (broken archive/compile) rather than producing exploitable behavior, and there is no memory-safety or injection component.

confirmed.title: Markdown renderer emits source-derived names/types/doc-comments without any escaping (markdown injection; XSS when the generated .md is rendered to HTML)
confirmed.code: sb_printf(sb, "| `%s` | `%s` | %s |\n", f->name, f->type_name ? f->type_name : "", f->doc ? f->doc : "");
confirmed.trigger: Run `clat doc file.lat` on an untrusted source (Markdown is the default format, doc_gen.c:2184). Field/return types are read free-form (parse_struct_fields, lines 276-281: only `,`/`}`/`\n` terminate them), and doc text from ds_read_doc_block carries embedded newlines and arbitrary bytes. A struct with a multi-line field doc, or a field type/doc containing `|`, a backtick, or raw HTML such as `/// ` or a type `
confirmed.consequence: Deterministic malformed/injectable Markdown (broken tables, injected list items/links). Because the renderer performs no escaping, raw HTML embedded in a doc comment or type annotation is emitted verbatim into the .md; when that file is later rendered to HTML by a downstream Markdown processor that allows raw HTML (MkDocs, Pandoc, marked.js without sanitize, etc.), it yields stored XSS. This is an asymmetry/oversight: the JSON renderer (sb_append_json_str) and HTML renderer (sb_append_html_esc, escaping & < > ") both neutralize the same source-derived strings, but render_markdown neutralizes nothing.
confirmed.verifier_notes: [high/low] Confirmed by reading the code. render_markdown (src/doc_gen.c:1429-1605) interpolates source-derived names/types/doc-comments verbatim with no escaping: the struct-field table row at line 1492 `sb_printf(sb, "| `%s` | `%s` | %s | ", f->name, f->type_name?:"", f->doc?:"")`, plus module_doc (1436) and doc/type appends at 1464,1468,1483,1509,1518,1520,1537,1549,1564,1578,1594,1597. The source strings carry arbitrary bytes: ds_read_doc_block (128-170) copies every byte to EOL into the doc (line 159) and joins multi-line `///` blocks with embedded ` ` (155); parse_struct_fields reads the field type free-form, terminating only on `,`/`}`/` ` (276-281). So `|`, backticks, embedded newlines, or raw HTML (`
